elements.sent-2411-001: The Bethe-Salpeter equation has been extensively employed to compute the two-body electron-hole propagator and its poles which correspond to the neutral excitation energies of the system.
elements.sent-2411-002: Through a different time-ordering, the two-body Green's function can also describe the propagation of two electrons or two holes.
elements.sent-2411-003: The corresponding poles are the double ionization potentials and double electron affinities of the system.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, a Bethe-Salpeter equation for the two-body particle-particle propagator is derived within the linear-response formalism using a pairing field and anomalous propagators.
elements.sent-2411-005: This framework allows us to compute kernels corresponding to different self-energy approximations ($GW$, $T$-matrix, and second-Born) as in the usual electron-hole case.
elements.sent-2411-006: The performance of these various kernels is gauged for singlet and triplet valence double ionization potentials using a set of 23 small molecules.
elements.sent-2411-007: The description of double core hole states is also analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-008: Despite its conceptual simplicity, the electron-hole (eh) random-phase approximation (RPA), introduced by Bohm and Pines in the context of the uniform electron gas, \cite{Bohm_1951,Pines_1952,Bohm_1953,Nozieres_1958,Gell-Mann_1957 has proven effective in describing a variety of physical phenomena.\footnote{The aforementioned RPA formalism is usually referred to as particle-hole (ph) RPA.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: However, for notational consistency, we prefer the terminology eh-RPA in the present context.
elements.sent-2411-010: These include collective excitations (plasmons) in metals and semiconductors, \cite{Martin_2016 non-covalent interaction energies, \cite{Lu_2009,Zhu_2010,Nguyen_2020 and screening of the Coulomb interaction due to density fluctuations.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Since its inception, the eh-RPA equations have been derived through various formalisms such as Rowe's equation of motion (EOM), \cite{Rowe_1968,Rowe_1968a,Mertins_1996,Simons_2005 time-dependent density-functional theory (TDDFT), \cite{Runge_1984,UlrichBook and the Bethe-Salpeter equation (BSE).}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Amongst these, TDDFT has been by far the most popular, though it suffers from well-documented drawbacks.
elements.sent-2411-013: In contrast, EOM can be systematically improved by increasing the rank of the excitation operator or moving beyond a mean-field reference.
elements.sent-2411-014: To go beyond eh-RPA, one must improve the kernel using, for example, perturbation theory or Hedin's equations.

elements.sent-2411-015: The eh-RPA possesses an analog known as particle-particle (pp) RPA or pairing vibration approximation.
elements.sent-2411-016: The pp propagator captures complementary information to the polarization propagator as its poles correspond to the double ionization potentials (DIPs) and double electron affinities (DEAs) of the $N$-electron system.
elements.sent-2411-017: In a recent series of papers, Weitao Yang's group has shown that pp-RPA offers an alternative approach to accessing excited states in $N$-electron systems.
elements.sent-2411-018: Yang and co-workers have shown that pp-RPA has some advantages over eh-RPA and TDDFT, such as the ability to describe charge transfer, \cite{Yang_2013b,Yang_2017a doubly excited states, \cite{Yang_2013b or conical intersections.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: As with eh-RPA, pp-RPA can be derived and extended through various theoretical frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-020: The EOM in the $(N+2)$- and ($N-2)$-electron sectors of Fock space leads to pp-RPA and, as for the $N$-electron case, the systematically-improvable DIP-EOM-CC and DEA-EOM-CC formalisms can provide reliable reference energies.
elements.sent-2411-021: On the other hand, improving pp-RPA within the pairing-field TDDFT framework is even more challenging than in standard TDDFT.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, the number of functionals for the anomalous density remains quite limited \cite{Oliveira_1988b,Luders_2005,Marques_2005 and, to the best of our knowledge, these functionals have not been employed to derive kernels within pairing-field TDDFT.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Finally, the pp channel of the BSE has been much less explored and developed than its eh counterpart.
elements.sent-2411-024: Although the BSE for the pp propagator has been reported in the literature, its kernel lacks a simple form.
elements.sent-2411-025: In fact, it is expressed as a Dyson equation of the eh-BSE kernel, making it difficult to apply in practice.
elements.sent-2411-026: This new expression is fully analog to the eh-BSE kernel as both are expressed as the derivative of a self-energy with respect to a propagator.

elements.sent-2411-038: The eh correlation function $L$ associated with $G_2$ is obtained by removing the uncorrelated part of Eq.~\eqref{eq:ehG2 , that is, \begin{equation}\label{eq:ehL} L(12;1'2') = -G_2(12;1'2') + G(11')G(22'),\end{equation} where we have introduced the one-body Green's function \begin{equation}\label{eq:G1} G(11') = (-\mathrm{i})\mel{\Psi_0^N}{\hT\mqty[\hpsi(1)\hpsid(1')]}{\Psi_0^N},\end{equation} which describes the propagation of either a hole or an electron.}

elements.sent-2411-040: The frequency-space representation of this correlation function is \begin{equation}\begin{split} L(\bx_1\bx_2;\bx_{1'}\bx_{2'};\omega) = \sum_{n > 0} \frac{L_{n}^N(\bx_2\bx_{2'})R_{n}^N(\bx_1\bx_{1'})}{\omega - (E_n^N - E_0^N - \ii\eta)} - \sum_{n > 0} \frac{L_{n}^N(\bx_2\bx_{2'})R_{n}^N(\bx_1\bx_{1'})}{\omega - (E_0^N - E_n^N + \ii\eta)}, \end{split}\end{equation} with amplitudes \begin{subequations \begin{equation}L_{n}^N(\bx_1\bx_{1'}) = \mel{\Psi_0^N}{\hpsid(\bx_1)\hpsi(\bx_{1'})}{\Psi_n^N}, R_{n}^N(\bx_1\bx_{1'}) = \mel{\Psi_n^N}{\hpsid(\bx_1)\hpsi(\bx_{1'})}{\Psi_0^N},\end{equation} \end{subequations where $E_n^N$ and $\ket*{\Psi_n^N}$ are the energy and wave function of the $n$th excited state ($n = 0$ being the ground state) of the $N$-electron system.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: This representation evidences that $L$ is directly linked to the excitation energies of the $N$-electron system.

elements.sent-2411-048: In the previous section, $L$ has been introduced as a correlation function but it can alternatively be regarded as a generalized response function.

elements.sent-2411-051: The Schwinger relation shows that knowing the eh correlation function is equivalent to knowing the response of the one-body Green's function with respect to an external potential.
elements.sent-2411-052: The pp correlation function can also be expressed as a functional derivative within the linear-response formalism.
elements.sent-2411-053: This is done by considering the response of an anomalous Green's function to a time-dependent pairing potential \begin{equation}\label{eq:pp_potential} \begin{split} \hat{\mathcal{U}}^\pp(t) = \frac{1}{2} \Bigg[ \int \dd{(\bx_1\bx_{1'})} \hpsi(\bx_1) U^\hh(\bx_1\bx_{1'};t) \hpsi(\bx_{1'}) + \int \dd{(\bx_1\bx_{1'})} \hpsid(\bx_{1'}) U^\ee(\bx_1\bx_{1'};t) \hpsid(\bx_{1'}) \Bigg]. \end{split}\end{equation} Note that, in the presence of this perturbation, the Hamiltonian does not commute with the particle number operator.
elements.sent-2411-054: Therefore, in the presence of the pairing potential, the wave function breaks the particle-number symmetry and the anomalous Green's functions do not vanish anymore.
elements.sent-2411-055: Following a derivation similar to the one of Eq.~\eqref{eq:eh_schwinger (see \SupInf of Ref.~\onlinecite{Marie_2024b ), one can obtain \begin{equation}\label{eq:pp_schwinger} K(12;1'2') = \eval{\fdv{G^\ee(1'2')}{U^\hh(12)}}_{U=0},\end{equation} with $U^\hh(12) = U^\hh(\bx_{1}\bx_{2 ;t_1)\delta(t_{1 - t_{2 )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: This expression shows that the response of an anomalous propagator to a pairing field (evaluated at $U=0$) is non-zero even if the equilibrium Hamiltonian preserves the number of particles.
elements.sent-2411-057: Therefore, through their derivatives, anomalous quantities can be useful even in a number-conserving framework.
elements.sent-2411-058: This was already highlighted in pairing-field TDDFT, \cite{vanAggelen_2013,vanAggelen_2014 and will be further evidenced by the pp-BSE.}

elements.sent-2411-250: This work provides an in-depth discussion of the eh and pp components of the two-body propagator, highlighting their similarities from the perspectives of both correlation functions and linear response theory.
elements.sent-2411-251: In particular, it demonstrates that the response of an anomalous propagator to a pairing field yields a kernel expression for the pp-BSE that is fully analogous to the standard eh-BSE kernel.
elements.sent-2411-252: This kernel has been explicitly calculated for common self-energy approximations, including the first- and second-order Coulomb self-energies.
elements.sent-2411-253: Additionally, approximate kernels derived from effective interactions, specifically $W$ (based on bubble diagrams) and $T$ (based on pp-ladder diagrams), are discussed in detail.
elements.sent-2411-254: These approximate pp kernels hold potential in other contexts, such as the three-body propagator equations, which usually rely on the Coulomb kernel or ad hoc kernels.
elements.sent-2411-255: The influence of the choice of the starting point and of the kernels has been investigated across a set of 46 DIPs.
elements.sent-2411-256: It has been shown that the static $GW$ kernel under the TDA is only slightly worse than DIP-EOM-CCSD but with a much lower computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-257: Furthermore, adding a perturbative correction on top of the pp-BSE brings it even closer to the DIP-EOM-CCSD accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-258: On the other hand, the second-order Coulomb kernel performs poorly for DIPs.
elements.sent-2411-259: However, the second-order kernel relying on the pp $T$-matrix effective interaction is more accurate than ppBSE@$GW$ and DIP-EOM-CCSD on average, albeit with a higher computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-260: Finally, it has been shown that the $GW$ kernel also provides a quantitative improvement over pp-RPA for core DIPs.
elements.sent-2411-261: While this work has focused on DIPs, the accuracy of the various kernels for DEAs is an obvious and interesting follow-up of this work.
elements.sent-2411-262: However, systems capable of binding two electrons are generally spatially large, posing challenges for our current computational implementations.
elements.sent-2411-263: In addition, the DEAs of the ($N-2$)-electron system computed at the pp-RPA level have been extensively used to compute neutral excitation energies of the $N$-electron system.
